topic.title: HTML Introduction & Web Basics
topic.description: A beginner's guide to HTML, how the web works, and the roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

content.text: What is HTML and Why Does It Matter?

text.value: . It is the standard language used to create and structure content on the web. Every webpage you visit — from a news article to an e-commerce store — is built on an HTML foundation. Learn more at the

content.title: Key Idea
content.text: HTML provides structure and meaning. CSS handles appearance. JavaScript adds behavior. All three work together but each has a distinct, separate job.
How a Web Page Reaches Your Browser

items: You type a URL into the browser address bar.
The browser sends a DNS request to resolve the domain to an IP address.
The browser sends an HTTP or HTTPS request to the web server at that IP.
The server responds with an HTML file.
The browser parses the HTML and requests linked CSS, JS, and image files.
The browser renders the fully assembled page on your screen.

content.text: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript: Roles at a Glance

examples.title: Your First HTML Document
examples.description: This is the minimum structure every HTML page must have. Type this exactly, open it in a browser, and you will see your first webpage. Every line has one job — do not skip any of them.
examples.code_snippet:
Hello, World!

This is my first HTML page.
examples.output: The browser tab shows 'My First Page'. The page displays a large bold heading that says 'Hello, World!' and below it a line of normal text that says 'This is my first HTML page.'

examples.title: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript: Each Doing Its Own Job
examples.description: This shows all three layers in the simplest possible way. HTML creates the button and message. JavaScript makes the button work when clicked. Notice there is no CSS — the page still works perfectly without it.
examples.code_snippet: Click the button
examples.output: The page shows a heading 'Click the button', a plain browser-default button labelled 'Say Hello', and an empty paragraph. When you click the button, the paragraph fills with the text 'Hello from JavaScript!'

subtopics.title: What is HTML?
subtopics.description: HTML is the markup language that defines the structure and meaning of all web page content.

text.value: wrapped in angle brackets to label and organize content. Browsers read these tags and render them as headings, paragraphs, links, images, and more. The full HTML specification is maintained by the

content.title: What HyperText Means
content.text: HyperText refers to text that links to other text or resources. This linking ability is what turns a collection of pages into the interconnected World Wide Web.

examples.title: Anatomy of an HTML Element
examples.description: Every HTML element follows the same simple pattern: an opening tag, some content, and a closing tag. This example shows the four parts of an element and introduces void elements that have no closing tag.
examples.code_snippet:
This is a paragraph.

Visit MDN
examples.output: The page shows: a line of text 'This is a paragraph.', a clickable link 'Visit MDN', a broken image placeholder with alt text 'Site logo' (because logo.png does not exist locally), and a pre-checked checkbox.

text.value: An HTML tag is like a
. The label does not change what is inside the box, but it tells the delivery system, the recipient, and every handler in between exactly what kind of item it is and how to treat it. A
tells the browser: treat this text as a top-level title.
. It does not perform calculations or make decisions. It
, the same way an editor marks up a manuscript with margin notes saying 'this is the title' or 'this is a footnote'.
to look up any tag. Each entry shows the element's purpose, valid attributes, browser compatibility, and live examples — far more reliable than memory alone.

subtopics.title: How the Web Works
subtopics.description: The web works through HTTP requests and responses exchanged between browsers, DNS, and servers.

text.value: When you enter a URL in your browser, it triggers a chain of events. The browser contacts a
to convert the domain name into an IP address, then sends an
request to the web server at that address. The server responds with an HTML file, which the browser parses and renders. Learn how this works in depth at

rows: Types address into the browser bar
Domain name resolved to an IP address
Sends GET request to the server IP
Returns HTML file with status code 200
Fetches linked CSS, JS, and images
Parses HTML and paints the page on screen

examples.title: What the Browser Actually Receives
examples.description: When your browser requests a webpage, the server sends back a plain HTML file that looks exactly like this. The browser reads it top to bottom and builds the page you see on screen. There is nothing magic — it is just text with tags.
examples.code_snippet:
Welcome to My Blog

This file was sent by the server.

About Me
examples.output: The browser tab shows 'My Blog'. The page displays a large heading 'Welcome to My Blog', a paragraph 'This file was sent by the server.', and a clickable link 'About Me' that would navigate to /about.

are human-friendly aliases. DNS translates them to numeric IP addresses like
that routers use to actually route the traffic. Without DNS you would need to memorize IP addresses instead of domain names.
Open browser DevTools and go to the
, then refresh any webpage. Every HTTP request the browser makes is listed with its
, response time, and file size. This is the fastest way to understand what actually happens when a page loads and to debug slow or broken resources.

subtopics.title: HTML, CSS & JavaScript: Separation of Concerns
subtopics.description: Separation of concerns keeps HTML structure, CSS presentation, and JS behavior in separate layers.

text.value: Separation of concerns is a core web development principle.
defines how it behaves. Keeping these in separate files makes code easier to maintain, test, and hand off to teammates. Read the official
to understand each layer in depth.

content.title: Why It Matters
content.text: Mixing CSS and JS inside HTML files works in demos but creates unmaintainable code at scale. A designer can update a CSS file without touching HTML. A developer can update JS without breaking styles.

examples.title: What Each Layer Is Responsible For
examples.description: This is the simplest possible demonstration of all three layers. The HTML creates the content. The JavaScript adds a click behaviour. No CSS is used — the point is to see how HTML and JS each do one job and nothing else.
examples.code_snippet:
My Page

Status: waiting
examples.output: The page shows a heading 'My Page', a line 'Status: waiting', and a plain button 'Update Status'. Clicking the button changes the paragraph text to 'Status: updated!'. No styles are applied — the browser's default look is used intentionally.

text.value: Separation of concerns is like a
) decides what dishes are on the menu. The plating team (
) decides how each dish looks on the plate. The waitstaff (
) handles interactions with the customer. If the chef starts plating dishes, or the waitstaff starts cooking, the kitchen breaks down. Each role exists for a reason.
External CSS and JS files are
after the first visit. On every subsequent page load, the browser reuses the cached files instead of re-downloading them.
Inline styles and scripts cannot be cached
because they are embedded in a different HTML file on every page, multiplying the data each visitor downloads.
, components appear to mix HTML, CSS, and JS in one file. This is still separation of concerns at the
: each component owns its own structure, styles, and behavior as a self-contained unit. The principle scales from file-level to component-level separation.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Introduction & Web Basics | Beginner Guide
seo_metadata.meta_description: Learn what HTML is, how the web works, and the roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Beginner-friendly guide with real examples and production best practices.
